title: What Do You Want to Be?
summary: An adaptation of my personal statement.

sections.heading: "So Darren, what do you want to be when you grow up?"
sections.body: As a kid, the more this question was asked, the less I knew the answer. I was usually quick to say, "I want to be an astronaut!" But my interests changed every day as a child; I cycled between technician, architect, and scientist. However, as I became older, I soon realized I wasn't sure what I wanted to do, but hearing this question over and over drove me to find the answer.

During that time, my portable Nintendo DS filled my right pocket wherever I went. There was something about the sound of a hoisted flag at the end of every Mario level that gave me a special sense of accomplishment on the drive home from school. Years later, I found that I could share this experience with others.

Although an entire Wii console couldn't fit in my pocket, getting to enjoy playing it with my brother, sister, and parents made up for it. Mario was no longer confined to a 3-inch handheld device but expanded onto a 50 inch TV, and now I had other people to share it with. Over time, the moments in the living room became less frequent, but my desire to hoist the flag remained: It was then I decided that I wanted to take gaming to the next level by creating my own team.

Once my team was formed, the stakes rose. It was no longer my family for a flag but my team for the win. I discovered a love and passion for competitive gaming. Not only did it allow me to connect with like-minded people on a global scale, but it offered an opportunity for me to be part of a community that competed to make a name for itself. But even though I had found something I enjoyed immensely, the question still lingered: 

`"What do you want to be when you grow up?"`
In retrospect, I realize that my passion went further than competing. I was not only interested in the games themselves, but the technology behind them that was so intimately connected.

I realized that gaming was simply a means to an end. My motivations were actualized through games, but they were never the goal in and of themselves. Before I asked myself what I wanted to be, I learned that I had to ask who I wanted to be first.

sections.heading: Suddenly, the answers flooded through.
sections.body: I wanted to be someone who inspires, both to better myself and those around me. I wanted to bring people together, whether they were a family, team, or community. And I wanted to do this through technology.

For me, this meant advancing software that allowed people to be involved in networks of communities. It meant creating an escape to the worlds that I had explored as a child and that influenced the person I am today. I want to create those same worlds and even better ones for others to experience as well.

When I came to that realization, the question that once instilled within me anxiety and doubts now filled me with excitement—the kind that makes you feel like you can do anything you put your mind to.

 What do I want to be when I grow up?

 `I want to be a Software Engineer.`

A leader who looks behind the scenes to create a “world” in which people are brought together and inspired to do the same.
